diagrams.title: Classic Deadlock Scenario
diagrams.description: A visual representation of two threads (T1 and T2) competing for two resources (R1 and R2) in a circular wait pattern. T1 holds R1 and requests R2, while T2 holds R2 and requests R1, creating a deadlock state where neither thread can proceed.
elements: Curved circular arrow showing the circular wait
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the circular wait condition - one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock. This helps students visualize how deadlock occurs in practice with simple mutex locks.

diagrams.title: Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)
diagrams.description: A bipartite directed graph showing the relationship between processes and resources. Process nodes are circles, resource nodes are squares. Solid arrows represent resource allocation, dashed arrows represent requests. A cycle in the graph without a wait-for edge indicates deadlock.
elements: Resource nodes: R1, R2, R3 (squares with instance count)
Solid directed edges (allocation - resource assigned to process)
Dashed directed edges (request - process waiting for resource)
Arrow heads showing direction of resource flow
diagrams.purpose: Teaches students how to detect potential deadlock using graph theory. A cycle in the RAG indicates deadlock when resources have single instances, providing a visual tool for deadlock detection.

diagrams.title: Four Conditions for Deadlock
diagrams.description: A hierarchical diagram showing the four necessary conditions that must ALL hold for a deadlock to occur: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, and Circular Wait. Each condition shown as a colored block with icon and brief explanation.
elements: Mutual Exclusion block (red) - icon of locked resource
Hold and Wait block (orange) - icon of thread holding resource while waiting
No Preemption block (yellow) - icon of non-revocable resource
Circular Wait block (purple) - icon of circular arrow
AND gate symbol at center showing all must be true
Brief text description under each condition
diagrams.purpose: Reinforces the theoretical foundation of deadlock by showing all four required conditions together. Understanding these conditions is essential for learning deadlock prevention and avoidance strategies.
